texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: PAC Codes for Source and Joint Source-Channel Coding
texts.text: PAC Codes for Source and Joint Source-Channel Coding

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Abstract -Polarization-adjusted convolutional (PAC) codes, as a concatenated coding scheme based on polar codes, is able to approach the finite-length bound of binary-input AWGN channel at short blocklengths. In this paper, we extend PAC codes to the fields of source coding and joint source-channel coding and show that they can also approach the corresponding finite-length bounds at short blocklengths.
texts.text: Abstract -Polarization-adjusted convolutional (PAC) codes, as a concatenated coding scheme based on polar codes, is able to approach the finite-length bound of binary-input AWGN channel at short blocklengths. In this paper, we extend PAC codes to the fields of source coding and joint source-channel coding and show that they can also approach the corresponding finite-length bounds at short blocklengths.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: I. INTRODUCTION
texts.text: I. INTRODUCTION

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Polarization-adjusted convolutional (PAC) codes, proposed by Arıkan in 2019 [1], is a concatenation scheme of polar codes which greatly improves the finite-length performance. The idea is to apply a convolutional pre-transform on the uncoded bits before feeding them to the polarized synthetic channels. It is shown that using a proper convolutional transform and a powerful decoder, such a scheme can approach the finite-length bound (or dispersion bound) of the binary-input AWGN channel at rate R = 0 . 5 and code lengths N = 128 and N = 256 [1]-[4].
texts.text: Polarization-adjusted convolutional (PAC) codes, proposed by Arıkan in 2019 [1], is a concatenation scheme of polar codes which greatly improves the finite-length performance. The idea is to apply a convolutional pre-transform on the uncoded bits before feeding them to the polarized synthetic channels. It is shown that using a proper convolutional transform and a powerful decoder, such a scheme can approach the finite-length bound (or dispersion bound) of the binary-input AWGN channel at rate R = 0 . 5 and code lengths N = 128 and N = 256 [1]-[4].

texts.label: text
texts.orig: PAC codes were originally proposed as a channel coding scheme. It has been shown that polar codes also have very good finite-length performance in lossless source coding [5] and joint source-channel coding (JSCC) [5]-[7]. Although for the lossless source coding problem, our previous work has shown that polar codes with CRC-aided successive cancellation list (CA-SCL) decoding can approach the finite-length bound, for the JSCC problem, as far as we know, there has not been any finite-length-bound-approaching scheme in the literature yet. This makes us wonder if we can use PAC codes to achieve this goal.
texts.text: PAC codes were originally proposed as a channel coding scheme. It has been shown that polar codes also have very good finite-length performance in lossless source coding [5] and joint source-channel coding (JSCC) [5]-[7]. Although for the lossless source coding problem, our previous work has shown that polar codes with CRC-aided successive cancellation list (CA-SCL) decoding can approach the finite-length bound, for the JSCC problem, as far as we know, there has not been any finite-length-bound-approaching scheme in the literature yet. This makes us wonder if we can use PAC codes to achieve this goal.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The motivation for studying JSCC is that Shannon's source-channel separation theorem [8], which states that we can gain nothing from the joint design of source and channel coding asymptotically, only holds when the delay is unbounded. In the finite blocklength regime, JSCC is strictly better than separate source-channel coding (SSCC). When the blocklength is small, source coding will have a certain amount of residual redundancy. If the residual redundancy can be exploited by the channel decoder properly, the overall error performance could be improved.
texts.text: The motivation for studying JSCC is that Shannon's source-channel separation theorem [8], which states that we can gain nothing from the joint design of source and channel coding asymptotically, only holds when the delay is unbounded. In the finite blocklength regime, JSCC is strictly better than separate source-channel coding (SSCC). When the blocklength is small, source coding will have a certain amount of residual redundancy. If the residual redundancy can be exploited by the channel decoder properly, the overall error performance could be improved.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The have been some researches on polar code-based JSCC schemes for better finite-length performance. A joint decoder that combines SCL polar decoder and language decoder is proposed in [9], which exploits the redundancy of languagebased sources during polar decoding to improve error perfor- mance. In [10], it is shown that the rate of polar codes can be improved by exploiting source redundancy. In that work, source redundancy is simply modelled using a sequence of t -erasure correcting block codes, which lacks generality. In [5], we propose a joint source-channel polar coding scheme and a powerful joint decoding scheme which can break through the finite-length bound of SSCC at short blocklengths. In [6], [7], a similar scheme with different decoding algorithms are proposed.
texts.text: The have been some researches on polar code-based JSCC schemes for better finite-length performance. A joint decoder that combines SCL polar decoder and language decoder is proposed in [9], which exploits the redundancy of languagebased sources during polar decoding to improve error perfor- mance. In [10], it is shown that the rate of polar codes can be improved by exploiting source redundancy. In that work, source redundancy is simply modelled using a sequence of t -erasure correcting block codes, which lacks generality. In [5], we propose a joint source-channel polar coding scheme and a powerful joint decoding scheme which can break through the finite-length bound of SSCC at short blocklengths. In [6], [7], a similar scheme with different decoding algorithms are proposed.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: In this paper, we further improve our previous work [5] by adopting the PAC codes as both the source and the channel component codes. First, we extend PAC codes to source coding and show that they also outperform polar codes. Then we combine source and channel PAC codes in the JSCC scenario and propose a joint source-channel PAC coding scheme, which is shown to approach the finite-length bound of JSCC over the binary-input AWGN (BI-AWGN) channel at short blocklengths. To the best of our knowledge, this is the first scheme that can achieve this so far.
texts.text: In this paper, we further improve our previous work [5] by adopting the PAC codes as both the source and the channel component codes. First, we extend PAC codes to source coding and show that they also outperform polar codes. Then we combine source and channel PAC codes in the JSCC scenario and propose a joint source-channel PAC coding scheme, which is shown to approach the finite-length bound of JSCC over the binary-input AWGN (BI-AWGN) channel at short blocklengths. To the best of our knowledge, this is the first scheme that can achieve this so far.

texts.label: text

Vectors are also denoted as X a : b ≜ { X a , X a +1 , ..., X b } for a ≤ b . For a subset A ⊂ [ N ] , X A denotes the subvector { X i : i ∈ A} of X 1: N . A C ( A ⊂ [ N ] ) denotes the complementary set of A in [ N ] . G N = B N F ⊗ n is the generator matrix of polar codes [11], where N = 2 n with n being an arbitrary integer, B N the bit-reversal matrix, and F = [ 1 0 ] . δ N = 2 -N β with some β ∈ (0 , 1 / 2) .
texts.text: Notations: [ N ] is the abbreviation of an index set { 1 , 2 , ..., N } . Vectors and matrices are denoted by boldface letters. Vectors are also denoted as X a : b ≜ { X a , X a +1 , ..., X b } for a ≤ b . For a subset A ⊂ [ N ] , X A denotes the subvector { X i : i ∈ A} of X 1: N . A C ( A ⊂ [ N ] ) denotes the complementary set of A in [ N ] . G N = B N F ⊗ n is the generator matrix of polar codes [11], where N = 2 n with n being an arbitrary integer, B N the bit-reversal matrix, and F = [ 1 0 ] . δ N = 2 -N β with some β ∈ (0 , 1 / 2) .

texts.label: text
texts.orig: To improve finite-length performance, a common way is to use the CA-SCL decoding [12], [13]. The idea is to retain up to L most probable paths during the SC decoding process and use CRC to select the correct one.
texts.text: To improve finite-length performance, a common way is to use the CA-SCL decoding [12], [13]. The idea is to retain up to L most probable paths during the SC decoding process and use CRC to select the correct one.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: B. PAC Codes
texts.text: B. PAC Codes

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Fig. 1 illustrates the PAC scheme proposed by Arıkan. A rate profiler first maps the information bits d to an N -bit vector v . Then, the convolutional transform with polynomial coefficients vector g scrambles v and outputs u . u is then fed to the polar transform. The convolutional transform creates correlation between bits sent to different synthetic subchannels, which can be exploited by the decoder. The rate profile (i.e., how d is mapped to v ) determines the construction of a PAC code. Just like polar codes, the performance of a PAC code greatly depends on the code construction. Arıkan shows in [1] that for N = 128 and R = 1 / 2 , the Reed-Muller (RM) rate profile (which is used to generate Reed-Muller codes) seems to provide the best performance. However, for other code lengths and rates, this may not hold.
texts.text: Fig. 1 illustrates the PAC scheme proposed by Arıkan. A rate profiler first maps the information bits d to an N -bit vector v . Then, the convolutional transform with polynomial coefficients vector g scrambles v and outputs u . u is then fed to the polar transform. The convolutional transform creates correlation between bits sent to different synthetic subchannels, which can be exploited by the decoder. The rate profile (i.e., how d is mapped to v ) determines the construction of a PAC code. Just like polar codes, the performance of a PAC code greatly depends on the code construction. Arıkan shows in [1] that for N = 128 and R = 1 / 2 , the Reed-Muller (RM) rate profile (which is used to generate Reed-Muller codes) seems to provide the best performance. However, for other code lengths and rates, this may not hold.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Suppose the polynomial coefficients vector of the convolutional transform is g = [ c 0 c 1 c 2 · · · c ν ] . Then the convolutional transform can be represented by the upper-triangular Toeplitz matrix:
texts.text: Suppose the polynomial coefficients vector of the convolutional transform is g = [ c 0 c 1 c 2 · · · c ν ] . Then the convolutional transform can be represented by the upper-triangular Toeplitz matrix:

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Thus, the encoding of a PAC code can be written as:
texts.text: Thus, the encoding of a PAC code can be written as:

texts.label: formula
texts.orig: x = vTG N . (3)

texts.label: text
texts.orig: For the decoding part, Arıkan proposes to use sequential decoding, which goes as follows. The decoder tries to identify the correct path in the code tree by using a metric that tends to increase along the correct path and decrease as soon as a path diverges from the correct path. When the path metric falls below a threshold, the decoder backtracks and chooses another path. When there is no path above the threshold, the threshold is decreased. The path metric can be computed using a low-complexity recursive method, as in SC decoding of polar codes. However, the biggest issue of this decoder is that the complexity is not fixed since we do not know how many times the decoder will trackback. For various SNRs, the average decoding latency of sequential decoding can be orders of magnitude different. To overcome this problem, list decoding has been studied [2], [3], which is shown to be able to achieve comparable performance to sequential decoding. List decoding of PAC codes is similar to that of conventional polar codes, except that when decoding an information bit, the influence of its previous bits need to be subtracted.
texts.text: For the decoding part, Arıkan proposes to use sequential decoding, which goes as follows. The decoder tries to identify the correct path in the code tree by using a metric that tends to increase along the correct path and decrease as soon as a path diverges from the correct path. When the path metric falls below a threshold, the decoder backtracks and chooses another path. When there is no path above the threshold, the threshold is decreased. The path metric can be computed using a low-complexity recursive method, as in SC decoding of polar codes. However, the biggest issue of this decoder is that the complexity is not fixed since we do not know how many times the decoder will trackback. For various SNRs, the average decoding latency of sequential decoding can be orders of magnitude different. To overcome this problem, list decoding has been studied [2], [3], which is shown to be able to achieve comparable performance to sequential decoding. List decoding of PAC codes is similar to that of conventional polar codes, except that when decoding an information bit, the influence of its previous bits need to be subtracted.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: B. Source PAC Code Design
texts.text: B. Source PAC Code Design

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Inspired by the idea of PAC codes for channel coding, we extend this approach to source coding. Just like source polar codes, CRC can also be used in source PAC codes to improve finite-length performance. Fig. 2 illustrates the block diagram of our proposed CRC-aided PAC (CA-PAC) source coding scheme, where s is the lengthN source sequence to be compressed, and s comp is the encoding output. In contrast to PAC channel coding, for source coding the convolutional transform is applied to the polar-transformed sequence v = sG N , which generates a vector u , where
texts.text: Inspired by the idea of PAC codes for channel coding, we extend this approach to source coding. Just like source polar codes, CRC can also be used in source PAC codes to improve finite-length performance. Fig. 2 illustrates the block diagram of our proposed CRC-aided PAC (CA-PAC) source coding scheme, where s is the lengthN source sequence to be compressed, and s comp is the encoding output. In contrast to PAC channel coding, for source coding the convolutional transform is applied to the polar-transformed sequence v = sG N , which generates a vector u , where

texts.label: formula
texts.orig: u = sG N T . (7)

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Then only a fraction of u (denoted by H , referred to as the high-entropy set) is retained. Finally, CRC bits of v , denoted by c , are appended to u H , which completes the encoding process. Therefore, the encoding result can be written as
texts.text: Then only a fraction of u (denoted by H , referred to as the high-entropy set) is retained. Finally, CRC bits of v , denoted by c , are appended to u H , which completes the encoding process. Therefore, the encoding result can be written as

texts.orig: 1 Since we are considering fixed-to-fixed length compression, lossless recovery is achieved as the blocklength goes to infinity. At finite blocklengths, there will always be decoding errors.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The choice of H determines the construction of a PAC source code, similar to the rate profile in PAC channel codes. By simulations we found that the RM rule does not work well for PAC source codes. Thus, H is chosen to be the same as that for conventional source polar coding in our simulations. Whether there exists better choices of H is left for future research.
texts.text: The choice of H determines the construction of a PAC source code, similar to the rate profile in PAC channel codes. By simulations we found that the RM rule does not work well for PAC source codes. Thus, H is chosen to be the same as that for conventional source polar coding in our simulations. Whether there exists better choices of H is left for future research.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: To recover s from s comp , we first use a PAC list decoder to obtain a list of candidate estimates for v , denoted by L = { ˆ v i } and sorted according to their probability. Then CRC is performed on ˆ v i to select the best estimate of ˆ v . Finally ˆ v is inverse polar-transformed to retrieve the original source.
texts.text: To recover s from s comp , we first use a PAC list decoder to obtain a list of candidate estimates for v , denoted by L = { ˆ v i } and sorted according to their probability. Then CRC is performed on ˆ v i to select the best estimate of ˆ v . Finally ˆ v is inverse polar-transformed to retrieve the original source.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: C. Performance
texts.text: C. Performance

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Now we compare the performance of the proposed CA-PAC codes with the CRC-aided source polar codes in [5]. We consider compressing a biased Bernoulli source with P(1) = 0 . 11 (denoted as Bern (0 . 11) ) at block-length N = 128 and 256 . For the CA-PAC scheme, we choose g = [110101101011] . The CRC length is 8 for both schemes. In addition, we also compare with arithmetic coding in this example, since arithmetic coding can produce near-optimal output for any given set of symbols and probabilities. Because the output length of arithmetic coding is not fixed, for fare comparison, we truncate over-length codewords to the same fixed length in simulations. The results are shown in Fig. 3. It can be seen that the CA-PAC scheme outperforms the CA-SCL scheme of the same list size, and both of them outperforms arithmetic coding for N = 128 and N = 256 . Also, in both cases, the finite-length bounds of fixed-length source compression [15] are approached with a very small gap. The reason that some schemes even outperform the bound for N = 128 may be that the bound is calculated according to an approximation formula and is not accurate enough at very short blocklengths. This result reflects that polar-based source coding schemes have great potential in the short blocklength regime where traditional compression algorithms do not work well.
texts.text: Now we compare the performance of the proposed CA-PAC codes with the CRC-aided source polar codes in [5]. We consider compressing a biased Bernoulli source with P(1) = 0 . 11 (denoted as Bern (0 . 11) ) at block-length N = 128 and 256 . For the CA-PAC scheme, we choose g = [110101101011] . The CRC length is 8 for both schemes. In addition, we also compare with arithmetic coding in this example, since arithmetic coding can produce near-optimal output for any given set of symbols and probabilities. Because the output length of arithmetic coding is not fixed, for fare comparison, we truncate over-length codewords to the same fixed length in simulations. The results are shown in Fig. 3. It can be seen that the CA-PAC scheme outperforms the CA-SCL scheme of the same list size, and both of them outperforms arithmetic coding for N = 128 and N = 256 . Also, in both cases, the finite-length bounds of fixed-length source compression [15] are approached with a very small gap. The reason that some schemes even outperform the bound for N = 128 may be that the bound is calculated according to an approximation formula and is not accurate enough at very short blocklengths. This result reflects that polar-based source coding schemes have great potential in the short blocklength regime where traditional compression algorithms do not work well.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Although the performance gain of CA-PAC codes compared with CRC-aided source polar codes may not be so significant, the convolutional transform provides more opportunity for joint source-channel decoding, as we will show in the next section.
texts.text: Although the performance gain of CA-PAC codes compared with CRC-aided source polar codes may not be so significant, the convolutional transform provides more opportunity for joint source-channel decoding, as we will show in the next section.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: IV. JOINT SOURCE-CHANNEL PAC CODE DESIGN A. JSCC PAC Code Design
texts.text: IV. JOINT SOURCE-CHANNEL PAC CODE DESIGN A. JSCC PAC Code Design

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Since both source and channel PAC codes can approach the corresponding finite-length bounds at short block-lengths, it is natural to think that their concatenation can also approach the finite-length bound of SSCC. A more ambitious question is, can we design a proper joint decoding scheme for the source and channel PAC codes to approach the JSCC bound? In this section, we show that the answer is yes.
texts.text: Since both source and channel PAC codes can approach the corresponding finite-length bounds at short block-lengths, it is natural to think that their concatenation can also approach the finite-length bound of SSCC. A more ambitious question is, can we design a proper joint decoding scheme for the source and channel PAC codes to approach the JSCC bound? In this section, we show that the answer is yes.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Fig. 4 shows the block diagram of our proposed joint source-channel PAC coding scheme. The encoder side is a concatenation of a source PAC code and a channel PAC code. A source sequence s is first compressed to s comp using the scheme introduced in the previous section. Then s comp is encoded using a PAC channel code. The intermediate variables in this encoding scheme are shown in Fig. 4. In the rest of this paper, we use H to denote the high-entropy set in u s and I the information bit set in u c . We assume that CRC bits are placed at the end, thus
texts.text: Fig. 4 shows the block diagram of our proposed joint source-channel PAC coding scheme. The encoder side is a concatenation of a source PAC code and a channel PAC code. A source sequence s is first compressed to s comp using the scheme introduced in the previous section. Then s comp is encoded using a PAC channel code. The intermediate variables in this encoding scheme are shown in Fig. 4. In the rest of this paper, we use H to denote the high-entropy set in u s and I the information bit set in u c . We assume that CRC bits are placed at the end, thus

texts.label: caption
texts.orig: Fig. 4. Joint source-channel PAC coding scheme.
texts.text: Fig. 4. Joint source-channel PAC coding scheme.

texts.label: formula
texts.orig: u I c = { u H s , c s , c c } . (9)

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Note that in concatenated source-channel polar coding schemes [5]-[7], the polar-transformed source v s is directly compressed into v H s , where H is the high-entropy set of the source polar code, and then v H s is mapped to v c , the input vector of the channel polar encoder. There is no direct connection between the abandoned source bits v H c s and v c (except for the source CRC bits if used), resulting in little opportunity for joint decoding. This may explain why the scheme in [5] can only break through the SSCC bound a little at very short blocklengths ( N ≤ 128 ). In the concatenated PAC scheme, the convolutional transforms create more connection between v s and v c , which can be exploited for joint decoding.
texts.text: Note that in concatenated source-channel polar coding schemes [5]-[7], the polar-transformed source v s is directly compressed into v H s , where H is the high-entropy set of the source polar code, and then v H s is mapped to v c , the input vector of the channel polar encoder. There is no direct connection between the abandoned source bits v H c s and v c (except for the source CRC bits if used), resulting in little opportunity for joint decoding. This may explain why the scheme in [5] can only break through the SSCC bound a little at very short blocklengths ( N ≤ 128 ). In the concatenated PAC scheme, the convolutional transforms create more connection between v s and v c , which can be exploited for joint decoding.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: B. Joint Decoding
texts.text: B. Joint Decoding

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The channel decoding rule used in [16] for proving the achievability of JSCC is
texts.text: The channel decoding rule used in [16] for proving the achievability of JSCC is

texts.label: formula
texts.orig: ˆ s comp = arg max s comp P( s comp |C s )P( y | x ( s comp )) , (10)

texts.label: text
texts.orig: where C s is the codebook of the source code and x ( s comp ) is the channel encoder's output codeword given s comp as the input. The first probability in (10) measures the source encoder's output distribution, while the second probability is the conditional probability of the channel. After the channel decoder determines ˆ s comp , a source decoder then reconstruct the original source with it.
texts.text: where C s is the codebook of the source code and x ( s comp ) is the channel encoder's output codeword given s comp as the input. The first probability in (10) measures the source encoder's output distribution, while the second probability is the conditional probability of the channel. After the channel decoder determines ˆ s comp , a source decoder then reconstruct the original source with it.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The joint decoding algorithm used in this paper is similar to that in our previous work [5] for joint source-channel polar coding. The core is to find a method to estimate the two probabilities in (10). In practice, it is infeasible to calculate them for every possible s comp in order to find ˆ s comp . Therefore, we measure the following quantity during the channel SCL decoding process 2 to imitate the idea behind (10) while limiting the search space to a manageable size:
texts.text: The joint decoding algorithm used in this paper is similar to that in our previous work [5] for joint source-channel polar coding. The core is to find a method to estimate the two probabilities in (10). In practice, it is infeasible to calculate them for every possible s comp in order to find ˆ s comp . Therefore, we measure the following quantity during the channel SCL decoding process 2 to imitate the idea behind (10) while limiting the search space to a manageable size:

texts.label: footnote
texts.orig: 2 The list decoding of PAC codes is also successive cancellation in nature.
texts.text: 2 The list decoding of PAC codes is also successive cancellation in nature.

texts.label: formula

However, P( u [ j ] ∩H s |C s ) (written as P( u [ j ] ∩H s ) for short in the rest of the paper) cannot be similarly calculated because u [ j ] ∩H s does not contain the abandoned bits u [ j ] ∩H C s . According to our mapping rule and the law of total probability,

However, P( u [ j ] ∩H s |C s ) (written as P( u [ j ] ∩H s ) for short in the rest of the paper) cannot be similarly calculated because u [ j ] ∩H s does not contain the abandoned bits u [ j ] ∩H C s . According to our mapping rule and the law of total probability,

texts.label: formula

texts.label: text
texts.orig: P( u [ j ] s ) in (13) can be efficiently calculated using a similar expression to (12) (by deleting y in the expressions). However, when | [ j ] ∩H C | is large, this problem is still computationally infeasible.
texts.text: P( u [ j ] s ) in (13) can be efficiently calculated using a similar expression to (12) (by deleting y in the expressions). However, when | [ j ] ∩H C | is large, this problem is still computationally infeasible.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Our solution to this problem is to use a source SCL decoder to approximate (13). Instead of considering all possible u [ j ] ∩H C s , we only consider the ones in the source decoding list, as the candidates in the list usually are the ones with the highest probabilities. Ignoring those that are not in the list has only a little impact on the estimation, provided that the list size is large enough.
texts.text: Our solution to this problem is to use a source SCL decoder to approximate (13). Instead of considering all possible u [ j ] ∩H C s , we only consider the ones in the source decoding list, as the candidates in the list usually are the ones with the highest probabilities. Ignoring those that are not in the list has only a little impact on the estimation, provided that the list size is large enough.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Fig. 5 shows the flowchart of the joint decoding scheme. At first, a channel list decoder decodes u c with list size L c . The list of candidates is denoted as L . When decoding a non-CRC information bit, a source list decoder with list size L sc joins the decoding process to estimate the source probability P( u [ j ] ∩H s ) for each candidate in L . Specifically, when decoding u c,i ( i ∈ I and u c,i is not a CRC bit), the path metric used for path selection consists of a channel path metric, denoted as PM ( l c ) c ( i, d ) with l c being the index of the candidate in L and d ∈ { 0 , 1 } indicates u c,i = 0 or 1, and a source path metric, denoted as PM ( l c ) sc ( i, d ) . PM ( l c ) c ( i, d ) is calculated using (12), while PM ( l c ) sc ( i, d ) is calculated as follows

When decoding a non-CRC information bit, a source list decoder with list size L sc joins the decoding process to estimate the source probability P( u [ j ] ∩H s ) for each candidate in L . Specifically, when decoding u c,i ( i ∈ I and u c,i is not a CRC bit), the path metric used for path selection consists of a channel path metric, denoted as PM ( l c ) c ( i, d ) with l c being the index of the candidate in L and d ∈ { 0 , 1 } indicates u c,i = 0 or 1, and a source path metric, denoted as PM ( l c ) sc ( i, d ) . PM ( l c ) c ( i, d ) is calculated using (12), while PM ( l c ) sc ( i, d ) is calculated as follows

texts.orig: After the channel list decoder has generated the final list of candidates, the following procedures are performed to recover the original source:
texts.text: After the channel list decoder has generated the final list of candidates, the following procedures are performed to recover the original source:

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: · Sort the candidates according to their path metrics.
texts.text: Sort the candidates according to their path metrics.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: · Starting from the one with the highest probability, check whether it can pass the channel CRC.
texts.text: Starting from the one with the highest probability, check whether it can pass the channel CRC.

texts.label: list_item

texts.text: If yes, perform CA-PAC source list decoding with list size L s and check whether there exists a reconstruction result that can pass the source CRC.

texts.text: If yes, return the result. Otherwise try the next candidate in the channel decoder's list.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: C. Simulation Results
texts.text: C. Simulation Results

texts.label: text
texts.orig: We consider transmitting a Bern (0 . 11) source over a BIAWGN channel. The channel code length is N c = 128 while the source length is also N s = 128 . As benchmarks, the SSCC finite-length bound is plotted according to [15] and [18] by optimizing the source coding rate at different SNRs and the JSCC finite-length bound is plotted according to [16].
texts.text: We consider transmitting a Bern (0 . 11) source over a BIAWGN channel. The channel code length is N c = 128 while the source length is also N s = 128 . As benchmarks, the SSCC finite-length bound is plotted according to [15] and [18] by optimizing the source coding rate at different SNRs and the JSCC finite-length bound is plotted according to [16].

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The result is shown in Fig. 6. In this example, the source PAC encoder compresses a 128-bit source sequence into 100 bits, including 8 CRC bits. The channel PAC code does not use CRC, as we find that this setting optimizes the overall performance for this example. The channel decoding list size is L c = 128 , the source decoding list size during the channel decoding process is L sc = 32 , and the final source decoding list size is L s = 128 . It can be seen that the proposed joint decoding scheme outperforms separate decoding schemes significantly. Besides, the joint decoding scheme has broken though the SSCC bound in the high SNR region and approached the JSCC bound. Part of the reason for the poor performance of separate decoding scheme is that we do not use CRC in the channel coding part. While this setting is better suited for joint decoding, it is not optimal for separate decoding. If we optimize the compression rate and allocation of source and channel CRC bits, the separate decoding scheme may approach the SSCC bound, but that is the ultimate limit.
texts.text: The result is shown in Fig. 6. In this example, the source PAC encoder compresses a 128-bit source sequence into 100 bits, including 8 CRC bits. The channel PAC code does not use CRC, as we find that this setting optimizes the overall performance for this example. The channel decoding list size is L c = 128 , the source decoding list size during the channel decoding process is L sc = 32 , and the final source decoding list size is L s = 128 . It can be seen that the proposed joint decoding scheme outperforms separate decoding schemes significantly. Besides, the joint decoding scheme has broken though the SSCC bound in the high SNR region and approached the JSCC bound. Part of the reason for the poor performance of separate decoding scheme is that we do not use CRC in the channel coding part. While this setting is better suited for joint decoding, it is not optimal for separate decoding. If we optimize the compression rate and allocation of source and channel CRC bits, the separate decoding scheme may approach the SSCC bound, but that is the ultimate limit.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Note that the SSCC and JSCC bounds are not achieved by a single pair of source and channel codes. At different SNRs, the optimal compression rate varies for both SSCC and JSCC. To see how close we can get to these bounds, we also optimized the compression rate for each SNR and plotted the lowest achievable BLER of the proposed scheme in the considered SNR region in Fig. 6, as shown by the curve termed PAC Joint Decoding Achievability. It can be seen that this curve is very close to the JSCC bound for the whole SNR region, with a gap of about 0.2 dB at BLER = 10 -4 .
texts.text: Note that the SSCC and JSCC bounds are not achieved by a single pair of source and channel codes. At different SNRs, the optimal compression rate varies for both SSCC and JSCC. To see how close we can get to these bounds, we also optimized the compression rate for each SNR and plotted the lowest achievable BLER of the proposed scheme in the considered SNR region in Fig. 6, as shown by the curve termed PAC Joint Decoding Achievability. It can be seen that this curve is very close to the JSCC bound for the whole SNR region, with a gap of about 0.2 dB at BLER = 10 -4 .

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: V. DISCUSSION
texts.text: V. DISCUSSION

texts.label: text
texts.orig: In this paper, we showed that PAC codes are also finitelength bound-approaching in source coding and joint sourcechannel coding, just like in channel coding. The main drawback of the proposed scheme is the decoding complexity, as we use a list source decoder to estimate the source probability for each candidate in the channel decoding list. Nevertheless, we successfully showed that the JSCC finite-length bound can actually be approached with practical codes. How to reduce the complexity while not sacrificing too much performance is worth future research.
texts.text: In this paper, we showed that PAC codes are also finitelength bound-approaching in source coding and joint sourcechannel coding, just like in channel coding. The main drawback of the proposed scheme is the decoding complexity, as we use a list source decoder to estimate the source probability for each candidate in the channel decoding list. Nevertheless, we successfully showed that the JSCC finite-length bound can actually be approached with practical codes. How to reduce the complexity while not sacrificing too much performance is worth future research.
